coredata.dc:title: Spectroscopic studies on in vitro molecular interaction of highly fluorescent carbon dots with different serum albumins
coredata.dc:creator: Reshma,
Vaishnav, Sandeep Kumar
Karbhal, Indrapal
Satnami, Manmohan L.
Ghosh, Kallol K.
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent Carbon Dots (CDs) have gained paramount interest in chemical biological and biomedical fields. In this work, in vitro molecular interaction of CDs with various serum albumins (Human serum albumins (HSA), Bovine serum albumins (BSA) and Rabbit serum albumins (RSA)) have been systematically studied by various spectroscopic techniques. Spherical and monodispersed CDs with an average diameter across of 10 nm successfully fabricated in water-(Polyethylene glycol) PEG medium utilizing glucose as carbon source. Thermodynamic and kinetic parameters of CDs with serum albumins (SA) have also been studied. The binding interaction between serum albumins (SA) and CDs were come about because of the complex formation of HSA-CDs, BSA-CDs and RSA-CDs. The altered Stern–Volmer quenching constant K a at different temperatures and their comparing thermodynamic parameters ∆H, ∆G and ∆S were calculated. The binding location of CDs with SA is around site I (subdomain IIA) and substantial conformational changes at both secondary and tertiary structures level.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Serum albumins
Molecular interaction
Binding constant

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Microscopic study of characterisation of CDs
Effect of CDs on serum albumins fluorescence spectra and quenching mechanism
Binding interaction between CDs and serum albumins

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Normalized UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs, Insert: photograph of CDs under the radiation of white light (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right). (b) Fluorescence spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelength. (c) FT-IR spectrum of CDs. (d) TEM image of CDs.
TEM image of the CDs produced by the carbonisation of (a) & (b) HSA and (c) & (d) BSA.

Fluorescence Spectra of CDs-HSA (a) 295 K (b) 300 K (c) 305 K and CDs-BSA (d) 295 K (e) 300 K (f) 305 K, (g) Stern–Volmer plot of CDs with serum albumins and (h) Binding Constant plot of CDs with serum albumins.
Stern–Volmer plots of (a) CDs-HSA (b) CDs-BSA and (c) CDs-RSA systems at different temperature.
Van't Hoff plot of (a) HSA-CDs and (b) BSA-CDs.
Effect of site-specific probes to HSA-CDs system, (a) warfarin (b) ibuprofen. (c) The modified Stern–Volmer plots of HSA-CDs system without and warfarin or ibuprofen.
UV–Visible spectra of CDs with (a)HSA and (b) Benesi–Hildebrand plots using changes in absorption spectra of CDs with serum albumins at 300 K.
FTIR spectra of (a) HSA and (b) BSA with CDs (1.1 × 10−2 mol/L) and CDs (2.5 × 10−2 mol/L).
Associative binding constants Ka and correlation coefficient (R) for CDs-serum albumins systems at different temperatures.
Relative thermodynamic parameters for CDs-serum albumins systems at different temperatures.
Binding constant (K), correlation coefficient (R) and binding number (n) of the CDs-serum albumins systems.

head.ce:title: Spectroscopic studies on in vitro molecular interaction of highly fluorescent carbon dots with different serum albumins

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Studies in Chemistry, Pt. Ravishankar Shukla University, Raipur, (C.G.) 492010, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Forensic Science Laboratory, Raipur, C.G. 492013, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent Carbon Dots (CDs) have gained paramount interest in chemical biological and biomedical fields. In this work, in vitro molecular interaction of CDs with various serum albumins (Human serum albumins (HSA), Bovine serum albumins (BSA) and Rabbit serum albumins (RSA)) have been systematically studied by various spectroscopic techniques. Spherical and monodispersed CDs with an average diameter across of 10 nm successfully fabricated in water-(Polyethylene glycol) PEG medium utilizing glucose as carbon source. Thermodynamic and kinetic parameters of CDs with serum albumins (SA) have also been studied. The binding interaction between serum albumins (SA) and CDs were come about because of the complex formation of HSA-CDs, BSA-CDs and RSA-CDs. The altered Stern–Volmer quenching constant K a at different temperatures and their comparing thermodynamic parameters ∆H, ∆G and ∆S were calculated. The binding location of CDs with SA is around site I (subdomain IIA) and substantial conformational changes at both secondary and tertiary structures level.

ce:section.ce:para: An innovative class of carbogenic nanomaterials i.e., carbon dots (CDs) have been extensively applied in biological and biomedical fields due to their unique properties such as high photostability, resistance against photobleaching, tunable optical properties, minimal toxicity and superior biocompatibility [1–6]. The carbon dots gained tremendous focus on biomedical applications for understanding the molecular interaction between CDs and serum albumins in biological system [7,8]. Therefore, it is imperative to study the effects of fluorescent CDs on biological system, such as bimolecule and cells. Now a days interaction of nanoparticles (NPs) with biological system has turned out to be a standout among the most fascinating area of fundamental and applied analysis at the interface between biomedical and life sciences [9–11]. A large part of the investigations have been performed by using cell biological and pharmacological approaches. However the recent work has revealed insight into the molecular aspects of the biological activity of nanoparticles. The application of sophisticated procedures of molecular biophysics may encourage the explanation of the bimolecular interactions included which reveal the crucial elements governing the biological effect of NPs [12–16]. An essential aspect of protein adsorption onto nanoparticle surfaces is the structural changes in protein that may give about the adjustment of protein adaptation. Structural changes in the protein upon adsorption onto the nanoparticles surface may well prompt the presentation of novel “cryptic” peptide epitopes adjusted capacity as well as eagerness impacts [17–19]. Serum albumin (SA) is the most significant plasma protein in creatures. It is made in the liver and sends out as a non-glycosylated protein into the plasma, wherever it reaches a high concentration to the colloid osmotic force. The binding properties provide serum albumins a wide range of biochemical, clinical, and pharmaceutical applications [20–23]. Serum albumins are relatively greatly size (smallest unit weight of around 66 KDa) and less than zero charged proteins. Serum albumins are relatively greater in size (smallest unit of around 66 KDa) and less than zero charged proteins. Serum albumins copmposed of three helical domains, each domain comprised of two subdomains. The being like in full growth proteins are had among its part of 585 (HSA), 584 (RSA) and 583 (BSA) amino acids. The overall order minds and physical properties of mature, BSA and RSA compared to HSA are 75.6% and 74.2% respectively. Liu et al. [13] found that MWCT (Multi walled carbon nanotubes) could interact with bovine serum albumin (BSA) through mainly non-covalent binding force. Xiao et al. [9] investigated the interaction between carbon dots (CDs) with human serum albumin (HSA). The results indicated CDs can have the strong bind to HSA; it is suitable for effectively investigating the metabolism of CDs in human and the biological behavior of CDs to organisms. Xu et al. [17] investigated the interaction between CdTe QDs of different sizes and HSA. It has been proved that some semi-conductor QDs can bind to serum albumins, which in thus modifies the metabolism of QDs and changes the structure of serum albumins.

Herein, we systematically investigated in vitro molecular interaction between fluorescent carbon dots and serum albumins [Human serum albumin (HSA), bovine serum albumin (BSA), rabbit serum albumin (RSA)] (Scheme I ) by different spectroscopic techniques including fluorescence, UV–Vis absorption and FTIR spectroscopy to acquire information about the molecular interaction. The thermodynamic parameter of CDs and serum albumins has also been investigated. The CDs were synthesised by one-pot method using glucose and polyethylene glycol −200 (PEG-200) in microwave assisted technique [9].The conformational variations of serum albumins induced by CDs were further investigated by multispectroscopic techniques, which may probably predict the behaviour of CDs at the nano-bio interface. The present work provides valuable information regarding the mechanism of interaction of CDs with various serum albumins at the molecular level. Moreover, the present research serves as an important strategy for better understanding of the basic behaviour of CDs in nanomedical applications.

Glucose, polyethylene glycol-200 (PEG-200), human serum albumins (HSA), bovine serum albumin (BSA) and rabbit serum albumins (RSA) (Purify >99%) were procured from Sigma Aldrich Pvt. Ltd. Serum albumins were dissolved in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) and stored at 4 °C. The concentrations of serum albumins were determined through absorption spectrometer.

UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured on Carry-50 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Varrian). Fluorescence spectra were performed on carry eclipse spectrophotometer (Agilent technologies). FT-IR spectra were recorded on Nicolet iS10 spectrometer (Thermofisher) using KBr pellets. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) measurements were performed on a JEOL, JEM-2100F, operated at accelerating voltage 200 kV. pH measurement were made with basic pH meter (pH 700 EUTECH Instruments).

Synthesis of the CDs was done according to the literature [9,24]. Initially 1.1 g glucose and 15 ml of PEG-200 were taken in beaker; dissolves in 30 ml double distilled water. This mixture was kept at 200 W at 160 °C for 1 h in microwave oven. The resulting solution was pale yellow in colour, which confirmed the preliminary preparation of CDs. Their dialysis was done using a dialysis membrane of 500 cut-off for 48 h. For further application they were stored at 4 °C. The molar concentration of CDs was measured on the basis of molecular weight determined by UV–Vis.

UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs was measured from 200 nm to 600 nm in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) at 25 °C. UV–Vis absorption spectra of serum albumins, CDs, as well as HSA-CDs, BSA-CDs and RSA-CDs systems were measured in the wavelength range of 200–400 nm in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) at 25 °C. The reference is used blank buffer and sample cuvettes.

Steady-state fluorescence spectra of serum albumins with different concentration of CDs were recorded at 295 K, 298 K and 305 K in the wavelength range of 300–450 nm. The excitation wavelength was 295 nm and the scan rate was 600 nm min−1. The excitation and emission slits were taken to 10.0 nm and 5.0 nm, respectively.

FT-IR spectra were taken via the diffuse reflectance method performed on a Nicolet iS10 Fourier transform infrared spectroscope (Thermofisher) by using KBr pellets. The FT-IR spectra of HSA and BSA in the presence and absence of CDs at room temperature were recorded in the range of 1500–1700 cm−1. The FT-IR spectra of serum albumins and HSA-CDs, BSA-CDs, RSA-CDs system were recorded in the range of 1440–1740 cm−1.

CDs showed bands from 200 nm to 360 nm in the typical UV–Vis absorption spectrum. Likewise with carbon based fluorescent nanomaterials, an obvious UV–Vis absorption peak at 282 nm, which contains two π-π⁎ electronic spectrum of aromatic sp2 bond. Subsequently, CDs exhibited an obvious narrow, fluorescent spectrum with range 431 nm, shown in Fig. 1(a). The digital pictures inserted in Fig. 1, the diluted CDs solution was pale yellow under ambient daylight and displayed slight blue outflow under UV-light (365 nm).

Besides, an excitation-dependent fluorescent behaviour of CDs was shown Fig. 1. When the excitation wavelength (325 nm to 365 nm) is increased the fluorescent intensity also increased regularly and CDs are showed red shift persistently. The fluorescent intensity of CDs firstly increased and then decreased subsequently. At long last, we found most extreme excitation wavelength at 345 nm and maximum emission wavelength at 426 nm. The excitation- dependent fluorescent behaviour was linked to different surface states of carbon based nanomaterials. The stability of CDs was also determined by monitoring the fluorescent intensity at room temperature in wide pH range (pH 3.0 to pH 10.0). The CDs exhibited high photostability at room temperature in wide pH range.

The surface functional groups present on CDs can be dictated by FT-IR spectrum. As shown is Fig. 1(c). In CDs present the surface functional groups identified by FT-IR spectrum. As appeared in Fig. 1.the absorption bands at 3395 cm−1 and 2883 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of OH and CH bonds, separately. The absorption bands at 1351 cm−1 and 1456 cm−1 were showed to CH bending vibration, and the absorption peaks at 1067 cm−1 and 1101 cm−1 correspond to CO stretching vibration of CO bonds around 1644 cm−1 was also detected. These results revealed that these CDs were functionalised with COOH and OH groups [25–27]. The presence of oxygen containing functional groups on CDs makes it water soluble and facilitate its interaction with various biomolecules. Moreover, the TEM image shows that the CDs were nearly spherical with narrow size distribution and excellent monodispersity. The average size of CDs was around 2.1 nm (Fig. 1d).

The quantum yield of the CDs was calculated by following equation [28]:
(1)
Q =
Q R
I
I R
OD R
OD
n 2
n R 2
where Q is the quantum yield, I is the deliberate emission intensity, n is the refractive index and OD is the optical density, which is measured on a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The subscript R refers to the reference fluorophore of known quantum yield, herein quinine sulphate (QS) utilized. The relative quantum yield (QY) of CDs was inspected to be around 1.5% under 350 nm excitation in reference QS whose quantum yield was 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution (n = 1.33) shown in Fig. S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microscopic study of characterisation of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Investigation of quantum dots size with transmission electron microscope showed that CDs and CDs-HSA, CDs-BSA (Fig. 2 ) and CDs-RSA (Fig. S2) system are well around dispersed. The scale of the CDs and serum albumins system were ascertained to be 5 nm and 10 nm respectively, which revealed successful modification of CDs with serum albumins.

In TEM images just little structures ~3 nm in diameter are seen. Collaboration of quantum dots with serum albumins could prompt the development of additional quantum dot coating layer that prevents quantum dots from aggregation. Included covering layer is not visible in TEM images result of serum albumins are formed of light atoms that are not visible in TEM images. In serum albumins solution is recorded the average diameter at 8–9 nm. Sizes of particles in CDs solution are >50 nm in diameter, considerably larger than size of single quantum dots (~2–3 nm). This revealed that quantum dots formed aggregates and affirms comes about got with TEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs on serum albumins fluorescence spectra and quenching mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The intrinsic fluorescence of serum albumins are mainly ascribed to the lone essential amino acid (tryptophan) residue [28] and fluorescence quenching efficiency of serum albumins by alternative molecules were widely studied by steady-state fluorescence techniques [29,30]. Herein, the molecular interaction between CDs and serum albumins, the influence of CDs different concentration steady-state fluorescence intensity of serum albumins at 298 K was shown in Fig. 2. CDs were obvious that showed strong fluorescence emission under excitation wavelength of 295 nm. Furthermore, CDs emitted virtually no fluorescence under an equivalent condition, which advised that CDs could not have an effect on the fluorescence property of serum albumins at all. Interim, The intrinsic fluorescence intensity of serum albumins was quenched step by step with the rise of the concentration of CDs which shows that CDs quenched the intrinsic fluorescence of serum albumins mainly through concentration dependent manner. The concentration dependent fluorescence change indicated the potentially greater toxicity to serum albumins at higher concentration of the CDs. The result agreed with the Stern–Volmer initial one-dimensionally at higher concentrations of CDs.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism is sometimes classified as quenching and/or dynamic which can be investigated by finding out the impact of temperature on florescence spectra [31,32]. Generally fluorescence quenching constants decrease with the increase of temperature for static quenching, because increasing the temperature resulted increase in the diffusion coefficient and therefore it increased the quenching efficiency. Therefore, the influences of various concentration CDs on the steady-state fluorescence intensity of serum albumins at 295 K, 298 K and 305 K were shown in Fig. 3 Fluorescence quenching constants of BSA-CDs, HSA-CDs and RSA-CDs system at three temperatures can be calculated by the Stern–Volmer equation [30].
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K SV
Q

Herein F
o and F are the fluorescence intensities of serum albumins in the absence and presence of CDs, respectively. K
SV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant and [Q] is the concentration of CDs, respectively. Therefore, the value of K
SV can be calculated by the linear regression of the plot of Fo/F against [Q] in step with the eq. 2. Stern–Volmer plots of BSA-CDs, HSA-CDs and RSA-CDs system at three different temperatures were shown in Fig. 3(a) & (b) (Fig. S3) and the values were listed in Table 1 . That was clear the Stern–Volmer quenching constant K
SV values are decreasing according with increased temperature, which is proved that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of HSA-CDs, SA-s and RSA-CDs system were static fluorescence quenching.

For static quenching process, binding constant (Kb) and binding number (n) of small molecules with serum albumins was analyzed by the double logarithmic equation [30].
(3)
log
F 0
− F
F
= log
K b
+ n
log
Q

The plots of log (F0−F) / F versus log [Q], the number of binding sites (n) and binding constant K b was obtained. In Table 2 , the binding constant K b and binding sites n are listed for CDs related with serum albumins. In Table 2 demonstrates that the value of binding constant Kb increases dramatically with the expansion of temperatures, showing that the binding ability of CDs-serum albumins systems would be incredibly increased when the temperature increased. Furthermore, the binding number n was almost kept constant serum albumins-CDs systems in three different temperatures. This phenomenon revealed that CDs bound strongly with serum albumins as indicated by mole ratio of 1:1, and independent of the binding of CDs to serum albumins.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding interaction between CDs and serum albumins

ce:section.ce:para:
Generally, binding interaction between serum albumins and other molecules may contain hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interaction, vander Waal interaction and hydrophobic force. Binding forces can be determined by thermodynamic parameters, like enthalpy change (∆H), entropy change (∆S) and free energy change (∆G) [33].
(4)
In
K a
= −
ΔH RT
+
ΔS R
(5)
Δ G = ΔH − TΔS

Since we have recorded the isoelectric point of serum albumin is around 4.7 solution pH plays vital role within the structural stability and the surface difference in serum albumins. Moreover, the impact of pH comes about on the binding interaction between serum albumins and CDs are explored to reveal the electrostatic interaction. The influences of CDs with difference concentration on the steady-state fluorescence intensity of serum albumins at three diverse pH values (pH 4.0, 5.0 and 7.0) were shown in Fig. S4. The Stern–Volmer quenching constant K
SV values were calculated and listed on Table 4 . The Stern–Volmer quenching K
SV took almost constant at three different pH values, suggesting that the binding force between serum albumins and CDs did not contain electrostatic interaction. Besides, the electrostatic interactions between serum albumins and alternative molecules have higher concentration of strong electrolyte hinder [34–36].

As reported previously, the structure of HSA, BSA and RSA comprises of two homologous domains (II&III) and every domain is made out of two subdomain (A & B) to decide accurately through competitive dislodging experiments by utilizing site marker fluorescence probes of warfarin (site I) and ibuprofen (site II) [37]. For this, various amounts of the drug were added to a solution containing settled amount of serum albumins and site probes and fluorescence intensities. The association binding constant K
SV was analyzed by using Eq. (2).The outcomes appeared in Fig.6 indicated that the emission peak of serum albumins red shifted clearly and the fluorescence intensity of serum albumins increased significantly after the addition of warfarin, whereas both emission peak and the fluorescence intensity of SA remained almost constant when ibuprofen was available furthermore, the association binding constant reduced within the present of warfarin and ibuprofen. As evident from these results, CDs could be essentially dislodged by warfarin and the binding site of CDs was mainly situated inside site I of SA and the binding site for CDs and ibuprofen was an equivalent in SA, at last the CDs was in all probability bound to the hydrophobic pocket situated in subdomain IIIA (siteII).

Fluorescence quenching mechanism can be revealed further by assurance of UV–Vis absorption spectrum of fluorophore. On the off chance that the two spectrograms coincide with each other it belongs to dynamic quenching otherwise is the static quenching shown in Fig. 7 (a). The UV–Vis spectra and CDs (pH = 7.40). Generally they cannot confirm completely that indicated the formation of complexes between CDs and serum albumins. Additionally, would that be able to the quenching mechanism is static quenching.

The binding constant of the CDs -protein complex were also determined using UV–Visible spectroscopic method as represented the equation given below [35].
(6)
1
A −
A 0
=
1
K
A max
−
A 0
Q
+
1
A max
−
A 0
where, A0, A and Amax are the absorbance in the without off and at intermediate concentration of CDs, and at saturation point, respectively and K is the binding constant. The plots of 1/[ A − A0] provides straight lines (Fig. 7a and b) (Fig. S5) that additional shows the formation of 1:1 complex between serum albumins and CDs. The values of the binding constant obtained from the intercept-to-slope ratio of the Bensei–Hildebrand plot (Fig. 7b) for protein- CDs complexes shows that the HSA indicates more binding affinity shown on Table 5 .

The conformational variation of serum albumins under the presence of CDs by using the FT-IR spectrophotometer was shown in Fig. 8a and b. Serum albumins displayed two absorption bands, amide I band at 1652 cm−1 (CO stretch) and amide II band at 1542 cm−1 (CN stretch and NH bending mode). The height position of amide I band moved from 1655 cm−1 to 1657 cm−1 (1.5 ×10−2 mol L−1) and moved to 1658 cm−1 (2.5 ×10−3 mol L−1). Moreover, the amide II band shifted slightly from 1542 cm−1 to 1544 cm−1 (1.1×10−2 mol L−1) and 1548 cm−1 (2.5×10−3 mol L−1). These outcomes revealed that CDs affected not just the stretching vibration of CO groups in the protein polypeptides however also the CN stretching and NH bending vibration mode. For the most part the interaction of CDs with serum albumins actuated the rearrangement of the secondary structure of serum albumins and CDs with higher focus displayed more noteworthy effect on the conformational variety of serum albumins that in agreement well the outcomes got from fluorescence experiments.

The interaction mechanism between CDs and serum albumins were investigated using the fluorescence, UV–Vis and FT-IR spectroscopic analysis. The result shows that the fluorescence quenching of CDs with serum albumins belonged to static quenching. It was inferred that ground state complexes was formed after CDs interacting with serum albumins. The quenching constant decreased with the temperature increasing. By calculating and analysing the thermodynamic parameters, we can know that the combining force between CDs and serum albumins were mainly hydrophobic interaction. The strong binding interaction between CDs and HSA is compare with BSA and RSA. CDs-HSA is favourable for effectively investigating the metabolism of CDs in human, animal and deeply illuminating the biological behaviour of CDs to organism. This shows that high concentration of CDs alters the conformational variation in SA which indicates the hampering of SA activity in organisms to an excellent extent. Thus, these are directly dependent on concentration of CDs. Therefore it can potentially mark the toxic property of CDs at molecular hierarchy. These results make better understanding of the in vitro molecular interaction between SA and carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, which is much important for the further in vivo applications of carbon based fluorescent nanomaterials in nanomedical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are thankful to Pt. Ravishankar Shukla University, Raipur (C.G.) for providing University Fellowship. Financial support of this work by the DST-FIST [No·SR/FST/CSI-259/2014(C)] and CCOST, Raipur (Chhattisgarh) [No. 2744/CCOST/MRP/2015] and UGC New Delhi [No. 43-183/2014(SR)] are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2018.01.146.
